To my South African brothers and sisters,

I understand and respect your desire to protect your country and create opportunities for your citizens. Every nation has the right to address its challenges and seek solutions for its people.

However, asking foreigners to leave by 30 June is a very short notice for people who have built their lives here over many years. Many are married to South Africans, have children, jobs, businesses, and deep friendships within communities. Uprooting lives is not something that can be done overnight. A reasonable transition period, such as six months or more, would allow people to make proper arrangements with dignity.

As Africans, we are brothers and sisters born in different countries but connected by one continent and a shared history. Let us not allow the actions of criminals to define entire communities or push us into decisions that may damage the image of South Africa and the values of Ubuntu that the world admires.

While I support the goal of addressing national concerns, I respectfully differ on the approach. Let us handle these matters with humanity, dignity, integrity, and wisdom. The strength of a nation is not only in how it protects its people, but also in how it treats others during difficult times.

With respect and concern,
